description: From a concrete example, we may infer that something exists.

guides.text: $\exists$ is the **[existential-quantifier|existential quantifier]** — it says 'there exists' or 'at least one.' The counterpart to $\forall$: one says 'all,' the other says 'some.' The goal $P(a) \to \exists x.\; P(x)$ captures a fundamental principle: from a concrete example, you can claim existence.

If $a$ satisfies $P$, then there exists *something* that satisfies $P$. This is called [existential-generalisation|existential generalisation] — from a specific [witness|witness], you infer that something with that property exists.
Start by assuming the concrete instance: $P(a)$.

The rule `exists-intro` takes three arguments: a step number, the concrete term you're generalising from, and a fresh variable name. Replace the term `a` with the binding variable `x` to get $\exists x.\; P(x)$.
[assumption|Discharge] $P(a)$ to finish. From any specific $a$ that is $P$, existence follows.

unlocks.name: ∃-intro
unlocks.description: If you have a concrete example $\varphi$ involving term $t$, generalise it to $\exists x.\; \varphi$ by replacing $t$ with a fresh variable $x$. From the particular to the existential.
